content.assessment: No directly comparable international case of partial detracking (removing only the earliest entry point while retaining later ones) exists in the evidence. The closest comparators (Poland's single delay, Germany's tiered restructuring) both involved uniform rather than partial/staged tracking-age changes, so transferability of this incremental approach is uncertain, though the underlying logic of removing the earliest, most distortive entry point is broadly consistent with cross-national findings on tracking age and inequality.
opportunities: Could serve as a naturally incremental test case generating Hungary-specific evidence on whether age-10 selection alone drives a disproportionate share of the inequality effect, addressing the general limitation that cross-country analogy cannot substitute for domestic causal evidence
strengths: Consistent with the general cross-national finding that earlier tracking is associated with greater inequality; removing the youngest entry point moves Hungary's profile somewhat closer to typical OECD tracking timing

Retains partial structural distinction from the sweeping single-point reforms of Poland or Germany, potentially offering a lower-disruption transition path, though this is inferred rather than directly evidenced
threats: Risk of displaced demand concentrating at the age-12 entry point is not directly evidenced by any comparator case in the record, making its magnitude speculative from this lens

Retaining 8-year and 4-year tracks with similar selection dynamics risks reproducing Germany's post-reform pattern where tier-reduction reforms preserved underlying prestige hierarchies and stratification
weaknesses: No comparator country in the evidence set has implemented a partial, multi-tier detracking approach, so there is no direct precedent validating whether displaced selection at the next entry point (age 12) neutralizes the intended inequality reduction

OECD evidence that between-school stratification (not within-school variance) drives tracking-related inequality suggests that retaining two of three selection points may preserve most of the between-school variance problem
